moveAnnotations.annotation: We're opening with the king's pawn, claiming central space and preparing to develop our pieces quickly. This move controls the key d5 and f5 squares while opening lines for both our bishop on f1 and our queen. The move 1.e4 leads to sharp, tactical games where both sides fight for the initiative from the very beginning. Against the upcoming Alekhine Defense, this central pawn will become a crucial attacking weapon as we'll soon see.

Our opponent launches the provocative Alekhine Defense, immediately attacking our central pawn instead of developing naturally. Black's idea is counterintuitive but sound: by forcing our pawns forward with tempo, they hope to make our pawn center overextended and vulnerable later. This hypermodern approach requires precise play from Black, as the knight will be constantly harassed and must find good squares. We should embrace the challenge and advance our pawns with tempo, gaining space and time for development.

We're pushing forward with tempo, attacking the knight and seizing more central space. This advance gives us a strong grip on the center while forcing Black's knight to move again, allowing us to gain time in development. The pawn on e5 also restricts Black's pieces, particularly limiting the squares available to Black's pieces on the kingside. This is exactly what the Two Pawns Attack is about—we're going to build a massive pawn center and use our space advantage to launch a quick attack.

Black's knight retreats to its ideal square, centralizing while staying out of immediate danger. From d5, the knight eyes several important squares and maintains some influence in the center despite being pushed around. Our opponent is following the main line of the Alekhine Defense, where the knight often gets chased but eventually finds a good outpost. However, this centralized knight is also a target, and we can continue our pawn storm to keep disrupting Black's coordination.

We're launching the second wave of our pawn advance, attacking the centralized pawn once again. This move expands our control over crucial central squares like d5 and b5 while preparing to push Black's pieces further back. The c4 pawn also supports a potential d2-d4 advance later, which would give us a classical pawn center. By constantly harassing the knight with our pawns, we're gaining time and space while Black struggles to complete development—this is the essence of the Two Pawns Attack strategy.

Our opponent's knight retreats to the rim, where it's temporarily safe but less active than it was on d5. Black is following the main theoretical path, as the knight can potentially reroute to more active squares later via routes like Nb6-d7-f8-e6. However, the knight on b6 is somewhat out of play for now, and Black has spent three moves with the same piece while we've been gaining central space. This gives us a clear developmental advantage that we should press by continuing our aggressive pawn advances.

We're completing the signature pawn duo advance of the Two Pawns Attack, driving Black's knight to an even more awkward square. This move creates a massive pawn wedge in the center, severely restricting Black's piece activity and controlling key squares like d6 and b6. The Lasker Variation continues this aggressive approach, and now Black must decide where to place the knight—each option has drawbacks. Our pawn chain gives us a lasting space advantage and excellent attacking prospects, especially if we can quickly develop our pieces and launch a kingside assault while Black struggles with their cramped position.
